coredata.dc:title: Highly photoluminescent carbon dots-based immunosensors for ultrasensitive detection of aflatoxin M1 residues in milk
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Guangming
Liu, Chen
Zhang, Xingcai
Luo, Pengjie
Lin, Guimiao
Jiang, Wenxiao
coredata.dc:description: Here, a facile hydrothermal method was used to synthesize highly photoluminescent N-doped carbon dots, and the quantum yields reached 97.1%. Then, a label-free immunosensor based on the inner filter effect of carbon dots was developed for ultrasensitive detection of aflatoxin M1 residues in milk. The detection limit was 0.0186 ng/mL (equivalents to 18.10 ng/kg), which satisfied the most stringent maximum tolerable limit value of 25 ng/kg. Besides, the immunosensor showed a good linear relationship from 0.003 ng/mL to 0.81 ng/mL, and the average recoveries ranged from 79.6% to 112.5% for spiked milk samples, with relative standard deviations ranging from 6.7% to 13.3%. Compared with other immunoassays, the inner filter effect-based immunosensor incorporating fluorescent detection into conventional enzymatic cascade amplification systems and could be a reliable on-site screening method for aflatoxin M1 residue analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aflatoxin M1
Carbon dots
Inner filter effect
Immunosensor
Milk

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of highly photoluminescent N-doped carbon dots
Aflatoxin conjugations preparation and antibody production
Development of inner filter effect-based immunosensors
Synthesis and characterization of highly photoluminescent carbon dots
Principle of the inner filter effect-based immunosensor
Development of inner filter effect-based immunosensors
Real sample analysis and assay validation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the N-doped carbon dots. (a) The TEM image of the N-doped carbon dots; (b) The FTIR spectra of the N-doped carbon dots; The XPS of (c) the full-scan spectrum, (d) C1s, (e) N1s, (f) O1s spectrum of the N-doped carbon dots.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of the N-doped carbon dots at different excitation wavelengths with the inset photographs showing these N-doped carbon dots under natural light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right) illumination. (b) The absorption spectra of PNP and the emission spectra of the synthesized carbon dots under 360 nm UV light illumination.
Principle of the inner filter effect-based immunosensor for detecting AFM1 residues in milk.
The calibration curve of ALP-based colormetric ELISA (a) and inner filter effect-based immunosensor (b) for quantitative analysis of AFM1 residues in milk.
The average recovery and RSD values of the inner filter effect-based immunosensor for detecting AFM1 residues in milk (n = 4).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: RSD is the abbreviation of relative standard deviation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Review on several recently reported immunuoassays for detecting AFM1 residues in dairy products.

row.entry: Raw milk, pure milk, skimmed milk, yogurt

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: IC50 is the abbreviation of 50% inhibition concentration.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: LOD is the abbreviation of limit of detection.

head.ce:title: Highly photoluminescent carbon dots-based immunosensors for ultrasensitive detection of aflatoxin M1 residues in milk

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Nutrition and Food Hygiene

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Guangming Li and Chen Liu contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, a facile hydrothermal method was used to synthesize highly photoluminescent N-doped carbon dots, and the quantum yields reached 97.1%. Then, a label-free immunosensor based on the inner filter effect of carbon dots was developed for ultrasensitive detection of aflatoxin M1 residues in milk. The detection limit was 0.0186 ng/mL (equivalents to 18.10 ng/kg), which satisfied the most stringent maximum tolerable limit value of 25 ng/kg. Besides, the immunosensor showed a good linear relationship from 0.003 ng/mL to 0.81 ng/mL, and the average recoveries ranged from 79.6% to 112.5% for spiked milk samples, with relative standard deviations ranging from 6.7% to 13.3%. Compared with other immunoassays, the inner filter effect-based immunosensor incorporating fluorescent detection into conventional enzymatic cascade amplification systems and could be a reliable on-site screening method for aflatoxin M1 residue analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Aflatoxins, mainly produced by Aspergillus flavus and Aspergillus parasticus, are a class of structurally related toxic fungal metabolites (Wang, Niessner, Tang, & Knopp, 2016). Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1, Figure S1) is the predominant mycotoxin produced by several species of Aspergillus. Of all naturally occurring aflatoxins, the AFB1 is a worldwide health concern and the most prevalent and toxic of all aflatoxins due to its contamination of a wide range of agricultural commodities (Xue et al., 2019). When animals consume feeds contaminated by aflatoxins, the AFB1 could be metabolized into its hydroxylated metabolite, Aflatoxin M1 (AFM1, Figure S1), by the cytochrome P450-associated enzyme in the liver. Then, AFM1 could be secreted into milk, and a recent study found that a large variety of dairy products, such as whole milk, skimmed milk, yogurt, and cheese, could be contaminated by AFM1 (Zhou et al., 2019).

Nowadays, aflatoxins are a worldwide health problem, and they have a negative economic impact on agriculture through reduced marketing options for crops and adverse health effects on livestock (Ren et al., 2019a). Aflatoxins contamination greatly affect the economic value of the crops, reduce the efficiency of animal production and increase the cost of surveillance (Nguyen, Flint, & Palmer, 2020). The intake of a large amount of aflatoxins in a short time can cause vomiting, abdominal pain, edema, jaundice, acute liver damage, or even death (Wang, Niessner, Tang, & Knopp, 2016). Of all exposed people, children are particularly affected by aflatoxin exposure, which leads to stunted growth, delayed development, liver damage, and liver cancer (Gratz, Currie, Duncan, & Jackson, 2020). Thus, the World Health Organization and the International Agency for Research on Cancer classified AFB1 and AFM1 as Group 1 human carcinogens (Du et al., 2016). To avoid serious health hazards, the European Commission set a limit of 25 ng/kg for AFM1 in milk and 2 µg/kg for AFB1 in agricultural food intended for human consumption. The US Food and Drug Administration has set the maximum level for AFM1 in milk at 0.5 µg/L, and for AFB1 in cereal-based food at 20 µg/kg (Zhang et al., 2016).

The aflatoxins occur in ultra-trace levels (0.1 ng/g or ng/mL) in a variety of food products (Li et al., 2019a). Thus, ultrasensitive and feasible analytical methods are extremely important to ensure food safety. Nowadays, immunochemical assays are the most commonly available methods for the routine screening analysis of mycotoxins in a large number of samples (Zhao et al., 2019). During the last two decades, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), lateral flow immunochromatography, and other immunoassay formats had been reported for the determination of AFB1 or AFM1 (Rahman et al., 2019; Ren et al., 2019b). We also reported the selection of an AFB1-specific monoclonal antibody and the establishment of a sensitive ELISA to simultaneously estimate AFB1 and AFM1 in different food matrices (Jiang et al., 2013a). These conventional immunoassays generally employed enzymes as labels and suffered from low sensitivity, which limits their further applications. Recently, new fluorescent probes, novel immunoreaction carriers, and promising immunoassay formats have been reported for aflatoxin residue analysis. For instance, fluorescent microspheres (Zhang et al., 2016), quantum dots beads (Zhou et al., 2019), carbon quantum dots (Lin, Zhou, Tang, Niessner, & Knopp, 2017), and other nanomaterials (Ye, Lu, Duan, & Wang, 2019) had been utilized as new fluorescent probes in order to improve assay sensitivity. These reports typically conjugate nanomaterials with antigen or antibody as fluorescent probes. However, the bioconjugation process usually could affect the fluorescent property of nanomaterials and antibody activity, thus resulting in comparable sensitivity to that of ELISAs. Therefore, a label-free immunosensor was in urgent need of aflatoxin residue analysis.

As one of the emerging carbon nanomaterials, carbon dots have been attracting continuous attention among researchers (Li et al., 2019b; Jin et al., 2018). The construction of a carbon dots-based fluorescent sensor has been a hot topic over the past decade. The inner filter effect, photo-induced electron transfer, fluorescence resonance energy transfer, and other non-radiative fluorescence quenching strategies had been reported for the detection of analytes (Wang, Li, Zhou, & Zhao, 2019; Wang, Chen, Wang, Tan, & Liao, 2017). Researchers reported that the excitation or emission of the carbon dots can be efficiently quenched by the enzymatic catalysts in conventional ELISA systems (Dong et al., 2019). Until now, carbon dots have garnered much interest in the inner filter effect-based fluorescence assay and show great potential in quantitative analysis for various analytes. However, few reports focus on the development of inner filter effect-based label-free immunosensors, and their applications in the detection of aflatoxin and other food contaminants are still rare.

In this paper, a facile hydrothermal method was used to synthesize highly photoluminescent N-doped carbon dots. Then, free carbon dots with super high quantum yield were introduced into an alkaline phosphatase (ALP)-based ELISA system, and a label-free immunosensor based on the inner filter effect of carbon dots was developed for ultrasensitive detection of AFM1 residues in milk. Compared with previous reports, the inner filter effect-based immunosensors are free from bioconjugation between fluorescent probes and immunoreagents. Therefore, this label-free immunosensor format is more sensitive and straightforward for on-site screening of aflatoxin and other food chemical residues.

Aflatoxin M1 (PubChem CID: 15558498), aflatoxin B1 (PubChem CID: 186907), and other aflatoxins standards were obtained from Fermentek Ltd (Jerusalem, Israel). Standard stock solutions of each aflatoxin (1 mg/mL) were prepared in a methanol/chloroform mixture (81:19, v/v) and stored at −20 ℃ in amber glass bottles. Ethylenediamine, ascorbic acid, bovine serum albumin (BSA), ovalbumin (OVA), Freund’s complete and incomplete adjuvants, and carboxymethoxylamine hemihydrochloride were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Inc (St. Louis, MO, USA). The 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) substrate kit and the p-nitrophenyl phosphate (PNPP) substrate kit were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc (Waltham, MA USA). Peroxidase affinipure goat anti-mouse IgG (H + L) and alkaline phosphatase affinipure goat anti-mouse IgG (H + L) were purchased from Jackson ImmunoResearch Laboratories Inc (West Grove, PA, USA). Transparent and white opaque 96-well polystyrene microtiter plates, 12- and 96-well cell culture plates were purchased from Costar Inc. (Milpitas, CA, USA).

The instruments used in this study consisted of an Infinite 200 PRO microplate reader (Tecan Group Ltd., Männedorf, Switzerland), a Nexera UHPLC LC-30A liquid chromatography system (Shimadzu Co, Kyoto, Japan), an AB Sciex API 4000 Tandem Mass Spectrometer (AB SCIEX LLC, Framingham, MA, USA), an FEI Tecnai G2 S-Twin transmission electron microscope (FEI Technologies Inc., Hillsboro, OR, USA), a Vertex 70 FTIR spectrometer (Bruker Optik GmbH, Karlsruhe, Germany), an ESCALlab220i-XL electron spectrometer (Thermo VG Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA), a C-9920–02 absolute PL quantum yield spectrometer (Hamamatsu Photonics K.K., Hamamatsu, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of highly photoluminescent N-doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The highly photoluminescent N-doped carbon dots were prepared according to the previous literature with little modification (Zhu et al., 2013). The citric acid (1.05 g) and ethylenediamine (670 µL) were simply dissolved in DI-water (10 mL) under magnetic stirring. And then the mixed solution was transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave. The reactor was put into an oven and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. High-quality N-doped carbon dots were obtained after purifying by dialysis (Molecular Weight Cut Off 500 Da) for 24 h. Finally, solid N-doped carbon dots were obtained by a lyophilization process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Aflatoxin conjugations preparation and antibody production
ce:section.ce:para: AFM1 was first converted to AFM1-O-carboxymethyl-oxime (AFM1-oxime) by the introduction of a reactive carboxyl group from carboxymethoxylamine hemihydrochloride according to our previous reports (Jiang et al., 2013a). After separation using thin layer chromatography, the AFM1-oxime was used for conjugation using the active ester method according to our previous researches. The conjugates were dialyzed against PBS (0.01 M, pH 7.4) for three days, and were characterized by the spectrophotometric method. Then, female Balb/C mice were immunized with AFM1-BSA for monoclonal antibody production. The monoclonal antibody was purified from ascites by ammonium sulfate precipitation followed using affinity chromatography, and then aliquots were preserved at −20℃ until use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of inner filter effect-based immunosensors
ce:section.ce:para: First, an ALP based ELISA was established as follows: the AFM1 coating antigen (100 μL/well) was diluted with an appropriate amount of the coating buffer and added to the microtiter plate, and the plates were incubated overnight at 4 °C. After discarding the remaining liquid in the well, the plates were blocked using 1% skim milk for 60 min at room temperature. After another washing, 50 μL/well of AFM1 standards and 50 μL/well of diluted anti-AFM1 antibody solution were pipetted into each well, and the microplate was incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. Then, diluted ALP affinipure goat anti-mouse IgG (H + L) was added to each well after another washing process, and the plates were incubated for 30 min. After washing, 100 μL of ALP substrates were pipetted into each well, and the absorbance of each well was measured at 405 nm after incubation in the dark at 37 °C for 20 min. For the inner filter effect-based immunosensor development, the synthesized carbon dots were added to each well, and then the fluorescence intensity was recorded by a multi-mode plate reader for ultra-trace AFM1 residue analysis.

The AFM1 standard solutions were run in duplicate wells, and the average absorbance values of these standards were plotted against the analyte concentration on a logarithmic scale. The competition curves were fitted by a four-parameter logistic equation, from which the IC50 values were calculated in the unit of nmol/L for sensitivity evaluation. To determine the specificity, a cross-reactivity study was carried out under optimum conditions. The cross-reactivity of the antibody against AFM1 and other cross-reactants was calculated using the following equation: Cross-reactivity (%) = (IC50 of AFM1)/(IC50 of analogues) × 100%

The milk samples were purchased from local supermarkets and stored in a refrigerator. All the purchased samples were verified to be free of aflatoxin contamination using a well-established LC-MS/MS method. The milk samples (5 mL) were centrifuged at 15,000 × g for 5 min at 4 ℃, and the upper fat layer was discarded. Then, the defatted milk was diluted 1:5 with assay buffer prior to analysis.

The final validation of the proposed immunosensor was carried out according to the related content of the Commission Decision 2002/657/EC by evaluating its limit of detection (LOD), repeatability, and reproducibility (Commission, 2002). And verified blank and naturally contaminated milk samples were obtained from China National Center for Food Safety Risk Assessment (Beijing, P.R. China) to further evaluate the accuracy and precision.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of highly photoluminescent carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots could be obtained mainly through a wide variety of approaches such as acidic oxidation (Sharma & Das, 2019), hydrothermal synthesis (Jing, Zhao, Sun, Zhong, & Peng, 2019; Liu, Zhou, Yuan, & Wu, 2017; Wang, Tan, Zhang, Duan, & Zhu, 2019), ultrasonic synthesis (Peng et al., 2012), and microwave-assisted synthesis (de Medeiros et al., 2019). Among these methods, the hydrothermal method has been considered as the most commonly used method to prepare carbon dots on account of the advances of simplicity, easy operation, and low cost. Therefore, a facile, economical, and efficient method for preparing carbon dots has been proposed by simply heating malic acid and urea mixture. In this paper, N-doped carbon dots were simply prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal reaction according to previous work. In Fig. 1 a, the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image demonstrated that the as-prepared N-doped carbon dots have spherical morphology with good dispersion, high crystallinity, and a size distribution between 4 nm and 7 nm according to statistical analysis (Figure S2). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis was taken to study the functional groups on the surface of the N-doped carbon dots. The following were observed in Fig. 1b: stretching vibrations of NH at 3267 cm−1, C-OH at 3075 cm−1 and 2970 cm−1, the vibrational absorption band of CO at 1663 cm−1, asymmetric stretching vibrations of CN at 1050 cm−1, bending vibrations of NH at 1560 cm−1, and OH–--OC at 961 cm−1.

To explore chemical bonds of the N-doped carbon dots, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was taken. Full-scan XPS spectrum of the N-doped carbon dots clearly shows three peaks at 285.0 eV, 399.1 eV, and 531.3 eV, which attribute to the C, N, and O elements, respectively (Fig. 1c). In Fig. 1d, the C1s peak at 285.0 eV can be deconvoluted into three peaks centered at 284.4, 285.7, and 287.4 eV, corresponding to CC, CO, and CN, respectively. The N1s peak at 399.1 eV can be deconvoluted into 2 peaks (Fig. 1e), namely, 399.3 eV (CNC) and 400.2 eV (N-(C)3). The peaks at 530.6 and 531.8 eV in the O1s spectrum (Fig. 1f) were assigned to C-OH and CNC, respectively.

The fluorescence emission spectra of N-doped carbon dots were investigated to explore the optical properties of the N-doped carbon dots. In the wavelength range from 360 nm to 420 nm, emission wavelength shows the redshift while emission intensity gradually decreases (Fig. 2 a). A colorless aqueous solution of carbon dots can emit bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet (UV) light irradiation (inset in Fig. 2a). The quantum yield of the N-doped carbon dots reached 97.1%, which is superior to most carbon dots reported. Compared with previous reports, we introduced highly photoluminescent N-doped carbon dots with super high quantum yield into the ALP-based ELISA system, and an inner filter effect-based fluorescence immunosensor was developed for detecting ultra-trace AFM1 residues.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of the inner filter effect-based immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The inner filter effect phenomenon is an important non-irradiation energy conversion model, and it was caused by the absorption of the excitation or emission by absorbers in the detection system when the absorption spectra of the absorbers overlap with the excitation or emission spectra of fluorophores. Previous reports indicated that the TMB could emit fluorescence with a maximum emission wavelength of about 430 nm under UV excitation, while, the as-synthesized carbon dots showed a maximum emission wavelength of 435 nm. Thus, the output signals of the carbon dots could be disturbed by the fluorescence of the TMB substrate during the inner filter effect establishment. One prerequisite for inner filter effects is that the absorption spectra of the absorbents could overlap the excitation or emission spectra of the synthesized carbon dots. In this paper, the ALP substrate has neither background fluorescence nor strong interfering fluorescence. The maximum UV–vis absorbance spectrum of p-nitrophenyl phosphate (PNPP) (about 315 nm) has no effect on the excitation or emission fluorescence intensity of the synthesized carbon dots at 435 nm. While, p-nitrophenol (PNP), the PNPP hydrolysis product by ALP, has a great molar absorptivity at 405 nm, which has a good overlap with the emission spectrum of synthesized carbon dots (Fig. 2b). To further confirm the feasibility, we recorded the fluorescence spectra of carbon dots mixed with PNPP and PNP (excitation peaks at 405 nm), and the addition of PNP (0.5 mM) could quench the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots at 405 nm significantly, while the same addition of PNPP cannot quench the fluorescence.

Subsequently, the synthesized highly photoluminescent N-doped carbon dots were applied for sensitively and selectively assaying AFM1 residues based on the inner filter effect. Conventional ELISA systems catalyze their enzyme substrate into coloured products absorbers, which resulted in a low signal to noise ratio and detection ability. In contrast, the fluorescence immunoassay could obtain a higher signal to noise ratio and sensitivity by replacing absorbers with fluorescence. However, the fluorescence immunoassay sacrifices the signal amplification of enzymatic reactions, usually resulting in comparable sensitivity to that of the ELISA. The novel inner filter effect-based immunosensor combined highly photoluminescent carbon dots with cascade enzymatic amplification. The highly specific monoclonal antibody against AFM1 was indirect labeled with ALP and used as a signal probe for binding with the analyte in samples and antigen coating. And PNP, the oxidation product of PNPP substrate catalyzed by ALP, could effectively cause the fluorescence quenching of subsequently added N-doped carbon dots, which was applied for the quantitative detection of AFM1 residues in milk. In contrast to conventional ELISA and other microplate-based immunoassay formats, the established immunosensors have the advantages of simple operation, high sensitivity, and no need to link absorbers with fluorophors. The principle of the established inner filter-based immunosensor was shown in Fig. 3 , and it could be a reliable and promising tool for aflatoxin and other mycotoxins residue analysis.

In this paper, we synthesized immunogens, immunized BALB/c mice, and produced a highly sensitive anti-AFM1 antibody. And we also established a conventional ELISA for aflatoxin residue analysis. In previous ELISA development, the most frequently used enzymes are horseradish peroxidase and ALP. In contrast to our previous reports, we developed an ALP based ELISA system for posterior experiments, because horseradish peroxidase substrate and its oxide products would produce strong interfering fluorescence, which could affect the accuracy of the immunoassay. In this study, PNPP was employed as the substrate of ALP to indirectly determine the activity of ALP based on the inner filter effect.

Before the development of the inner filter effect-based immunosensors, we optimized the ALP-based ELISA system according to our previous research (Jiang et al., 2013b). When the working concentration of the ALP labelled goat-anti-mouse IgG was set to be 0.5 µg/mL, the suitable working concentration of the coating antigen and anti-AFM1 antibody was determined to be 0.25 µg/mL and 0.4 µg/mL. Then, a calibration curve was established, and from which we evaluated the sensitivity of the ALP based ELISA (Fig. 4 a). The half-maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) value values were calculated to be 0.13 ng/mL for AFM1, which indicates that the antibody used in this study had a strong affinity to the corresponding target molecules. Under this condition, it will be extremely difficult to estimate ultra-trace AFM1 residue in dairy product for orphans, due to the MRL was set to be 25 ng/kg.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of inner filter effect-based immunosensors
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, we try to improve assay sensitivity via the inner filter effect between the highly photoluminescent N-doped carbon dots and the cascade enzymatic amplification of the ELISA. Previous reports indicated that the fluorescence of carbon dots is sensitive to the pH of the assay buffer. Thus, we evaluated the fluorescence stability of the synthesized carbon dots in the assay buffer at different pH to avoid fluorescence quenching. As shown in Figure S3a, the fluorescence intensity gradually increased within the pH range of 4–7, and then dropped within the range of 7–10. The optimal pH for ALP activity was in the range of 8–10, while, the fluorescence stability was relatively good in the pH range of 4–10. Thus, synthesized carbon dots were acceptable for acting as the fluorescent probes in the ALP-based immunoassay formats. Furthermore, the highly photoluminescent carbon dots have excellent photo-stability and their fluorescence intensity was also very stable within 30 min during the inner filter effect process (Figure S3b). Some reports also indicated that the carbon dots may have enzyme mimicking properties. In this study, we measured the absorbance of the substrates with ALP and substrates with 2 μg/mL of carbon dots. The results indicated that there were no obvious absorbance changes within 30 min (Figure S4). The above results confirmed that the selected carbon dots were stable fluorescent probes during the ALP triggerment and inner filter effect-based signal transition and amplification.

For the quantitative analysis, a series of aflatoxin standard solutions were tested to establish the calibration curves. Under the optimized conditions, the calibration curve was obtained by plotting between fluorescence intensity and concentrations of AFM1. As we can see in Fig. 4b, the fluorescence intensity increased when the AFM1 concentration increased from 0 ng/mL to 8.1 ng/mL and saturated when the concentration reached 8.1 ng/mL. It confirmed that the highly photoluminescent carbon dots triggered the inner filter effects from the PNP and then induced fluorescence quenching. Then, we fitted the fluorescence signal gain to a dynamic four-parameter equation to obtain an apparent equilibrium dissociation constant (IC50) of 0.026 ng/mL. The fluorescence signals showed a linear relationship (R = 0.994) with the logarithmic concentrations of AFM1 from 0.003 to 0.81 ng/mL (inset of Fig. 4b).

The assay sensitivity was expressed by IC50, which was measured to be 0.026 ng/mL. Then, the calibration curves resulted from three different batches were tested, and the IC50 values were 0.024, 0.025, and 0.028 ng/mL, respectively. These results demonstrated the assay sensitivity was reproducible, which can ensure the development of a reproducible inner filter effect-based immunosensor for AFM1 residue analysis. Further, we evaluate the specificity by determining the cross-reactivity with a set of structurally related analogues. The fluorescence intensity increase remarkably in the presence of 0.05 ng/mL of AFM1, whereas other related analogues (10 ng/mL) did not cause significant fluorescence change over the blank signal. The established immunosensor showed negligible cross-reactivity with AFB1, AFB2, AFM2, AFG1, and AFG2. The IC50 and cross-reactivity values were summarized in Table S1. These results demonstrate the co-existence of the tested mycotoxins did not interfere with the detection for AFM1, and the inner filter effect-based immunosensor was highly specific towards ultra-trace AFM1 residues in milk samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real sample analysis and assay validation
ce:section.ce:para: For investigating the applicability of the inner filter effect-based immunosensor in facing up complicated milk matrix, the proposed immunosensor was tested for the recognition of artificial spiked AFM1 in milk samples. Previously, aflatoxins with a series of concentrations were added to the negative control milk sample which was pre-treated according to the sample treatment method and used as the working buffer to establish the calibration curves for quantifying aflatoxin residues. However, the matrix spiked calibration curves make the new immunoassay formats complicated and unsuitable for on-site screening purposes.

Matrix interferences in milk is a great challenge for on-site screening tests. In this paper, the milk samples were purchased from a local vendor. We compared the analytical performance of raw milk, diluted milk, and defatted milk in the established immuosensors (Figure S5). The slopes of the AFM1 standard curve in whole milk (582.74) and diluted milk (605.46) were lower than in defatted milk (628.42). This means that the sensitivity of AFM1 immunoassay in whole milk and diluted milk was lower than the sensitivity in defatted milk samples. Thus, the milk samples were defatted by centrifugation and then diluted five times for posterior analysis.

The limit of detection (LOD) of the established immunosensor was obtained by the mean value of twenty blank samples plus three times the standard deviation. According to the practical detection results, the detection limit of AFM1 in raw milk was 0.0186 ng/mL (18.6 ng/L equivalents to 18.10 ng/kg), which has satisfied the most stringent maximum tolerable limit value of 25 ng/kg in milk by EU and US. Besides, the results were better in the assay buffer because of the absence of fat present in whole bovine milk and raw milk.

The accuracy and reproducibility of inner filter effect-based immunosensor were important for the development of a new analytical method. To verify the applicability of the proposed method, we investigated the accuracy and reproducibility of our strategy by spiking three AFM1 levels (low, middle, high) in milk samples containing 0.025, 0.1, and 0.5 ng/mL. Prior to sample extractions described above, each sample was spiked at three concentration levels and run in triplicates. No positive results were obtained for the non-spiked samples, and the milk samples spiked with AFM1 showed a good agreement with the concentration detected in the range of 0.025 to 0.5 ng/mL. The accuracy and reproducibility were evaluated by repeated analysis of the spiked samples to assess their recovery, intra- and inter-assay relative standard deviation (RSD). Experimental data in Table 1 indicated that the mean recovery for AFM1 ranged from 79.6% to 112.5% with RSDs in the range of 6.7%-13.3% toward the spiked levels.

Finally, naturally contaminated were confirmed by LC-MS/MS and then detected with the inner filter effect-based immunosensor for the final validation. In this study, two milk samples were used for the analysis of the correlation of the developed immunosensor and LC-MS/MS method. Concentrations of AFM1 in two samples determined by LC-MS/MS were 0.56 ng/mL and 0.123 ng/mL according to the procedure of GB 5009.24–2016, while, the concentrations determined by the inner filter effect-based immunosensor were 0.129 ng/mL and 0.0262 ng/mL. In addition, the dilution factor for milk samples was 0.2, thus, the corrected concentrations determined by the developed immunosensor were 0.645 ng/mL and 0.131 ng/mL. Therefore, there was a good correlation between the developed inner filter effect-based immunosensor and the LC-MS/MS method.

Food safety issues drive the development of highly sensitive and selective methods for aflatoxin residue analysis. Various analytical methods have been developed for the determination of aflatoxins in different food matrices. Of all, immunoassays are powerful for routine monitoring of aflatoxins residues in a large number of samples before confirmation by chromatography methods. During the last two decades, the ELISA and the lateral flow strip are widely applied for detecting aflatoxin residues in different food matrices. The horseradish peroxidase or ALP-based ELSA and the gold nanoparticle-based lateral flow immunochromatographic strip have higher throughput with fewer sample pretreatment procedures, and they have been extensively used for routine screening purposes (Jiang et al., 2013b). These immunoassay developments contributed greatly to the control of aflatoxins, however, most of these reports could not satisfy the ultra-trace AFM1 residue analysis due to its ultra-low MRLs of 25 ng/kg in dairy products. Nevertheless, the sensitivity of conventional ELISAs involving horseradish peroxidase catalytic tetramethylbenzidine as signal output generally ranges from 0.1 to 10 ng/mL, which is insufficient to reach the European Union standard for the rapid screening and detection of AFM1 residue in dairy products.

Immunoassays are powerful tools in bioanalysis, and most immunoassay reports for aflatoxin detection are based on the bio-specific reaction between an antigen and a selective antibody. In Table 2 and Table S2, we summarized recent advances and illustrate new probes, novel concepts, and promising applications in the field of aflatoxin residue analysis. Conventional immunoglobulins have been widely applied in immunoassay development, and the IgA (Ertekin, Pirinçci, & Öztürk, 2016), nanobody (Zhao et al., 2019), aptamer (He et al., 2020; Kasoju et al., 2020; Nasirian et al., 2017; Wang, Li, Zhou, & Zhao, 2019; Ye, Lu, Duan, & Wang, 2019), and other molecules were also tested to form bio-specific recognization for aflatoxins detection. Nowadays, these molecules are increasingly reported on new method development for aflatoxin residue analysis. In the authors’ opinion, these molecules could provide superior analytical sensitivity, however, their substantial stability and ability for real sample analysis were not satisfying.

During the last decade, nanomaterials with various shapes, sizes, and compositions, e.g., noble metal nanoparticles, magnetic particles, carbon nanomaterials, quantum dots, fluorescent dye-doped nanoparticles, and rare-earth-based nanoparticles, have been synthesized for food safety and biomedical applications. Recently, fluorescent microspheres (Zhang et al., 2016), quantum dots beads (Zhou et al., 2019), carbon quantum dots (Lin, Zhou, Tang, Niessner, & Knopp, 2017), and other nanomaterials (Ye, Lu, Duan, & Wang, 2019) had been reported in aflatoxin residue analysis. Compared with conventional probes, nanomaterials exhibit good biocompatibility, high surface-to-volume ratio, and unique physical and chemical properties. Furthermore, these nanomaterials had been widely applied as excellent candidates for the fabrication of new immunoassay formats. However, these nanomaterials were synthesized from heavy metal elements, and their toxicity remains a major concern. In contrast to other fluorescent labels, carbon dots are eco-friendly and easy to prepare, and they have tunable fluorescence emission. In this paper, we synthesized highly photoluminescent N-doped carbon dots, and they have a high quantum yield of 97.1%, which is superior to most reports. Moreover, the eco-friendly carbon dots were synthesized in an aqueous phase, which facilitates their application in immunoassays. In addition, we introduced the highly photoluminescent carbon dots into the ALP-based ELISA system, and an inner filter effect-based immunosensor was established to estimate ultra-trace AFM1 residues in milk. Hence, the facile preparation of carbon dots is particularly desirable for application in immunoassays.

Furthermore, some researchers use fluorescent microsphere (Zhang et al., 2016), magnetic nanoparticles (Su, Tang, Feng, Yao, & Chen, 2020; Sun et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2019), and other new materials as choices of solid phase to minimize matrix interferences and enhance the analytical sensitivity in different immunoassay formats. These nanomaterials were used as support materials for the immobilization of biomolecules to separate the bound and free fraction of the tracer. In addition, these new nanomaterials have been combined with microfluidics (He et al., 2020; Kasoju et al., 2020), electrochemical (Lin, Zhou, Tang, Niessner, & Knopp, 2017; Sun et al., 2020), optical (Sheini, 2020), and piezoelectric detection for aflatoxin residue analysis. Although the novel immunoassay formats have great contributions to the sensitivity in assay buffer, the complicated food matrices were still great obstacles for the robustness. Besides, the food matrices maybe cause false-positive results by quenching the fluorescent probes. In this study, the extracted food matrix would only interfere with the specific hapten-antibody recognization. In our previous reports and the supplementary materials, we select the assay buffer and optimize the sample pretreatment to reduce the food matrix effect to insignificant levels. Furthermore, we added the synthesized carbon dots to the enzymatic substrate after the colorimetric reaction. Thus, the carbon dots would not interfere with the food matrix. These methods are not compatible with the conventional ELISA platform, resulting in many obstacles in the industrialization process. While, the microplate-based immunoassays were easy-to-operate, and had been widely applied in aflatoxin residue analysis. In contrast to previous reports, the established immunosensor in this study incorporated highly sensitive fluorescent immunoassay into conventional enzymatic cascade amplification systems to enhance its analytical sensitivity. The European Commission has set a limit of 25 ng/kg for AFM1 in dairy products, and the LODs of the inner filter effect-based immunosensor were far below the MRLs set by the European Commission, US, and China.

In the present study, we developed an inner filter effect-based immunosensor for the ultrasensitive detection of AFM1 residues in milk by introducing highly photoluminescent N-doped carbon dot into conventional ELISA systems. The newly selected N-doped carbon dots exhibited super high quantum yields (97.1%) than that of the corresponding quantum dots, which is important to enhance the analytical sensitivity. Based on the highly photoluminescent carbon dots, the inner filter effects based immunosensor obtained a LOD of 0.0186 ng/mL for AFM1, which was 4-fold lower than that of the conventional colorimetric ELISA using the same immunoreagents. The lower LODs were more suitable for ultra-trace AFM1 residues in milk. Compared with previous reports, the established immunosensor incorporated highly sensitive fluorescence immunoassay into conventional enzymatic cascade amplification systems. The inner filter effects based immunosensor format is more flexible and straightforward without the link of absorber with the flouorescer, and it would be a promising immunoassay format for ultra-trace chemical residue analysis.

Guangming Li: Writing - original draft, Project administration. Chen Liu: Project administration. Xingcai Zhang: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing. Pengjie Luo: Validation. Guimiao Lin: Methodology. Wenxiao Jiang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31972763 and No. 31602103), Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province, China (No. 2019A1515011834), Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangdong Province, China (No. 2017B020207009), Shenzhen Basic Research Projects (No. JCYJ20190808160003705 and No. JCYJ20170817095240632) andmedical youngscientistsprogram in Shenzhen University.
The animal experiments were performed with the approval of the Institutional Authority for Laboratory Animal Care from the Animal Ethical and Welfare Committee of Shenzhen University.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2021.129443.
